exemplar.title: M25 #2: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #2: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Sustained, focused engagement with the prescribed title throughout the discussion
Effective and purposeful links to both the arts and mathematics areas of knowledge
Clear, coherent arguments supported by wellâchosen, specific examples (e.g. The Last Supper, the Yamabe problem)
Evidence of awareness of opposing points of view and some evaluation of their implications
Definitions and application of ârevered knowledgeâ are consistently integrated into the analysis
weaknesses: Evaluation of counter-claims is sometimes asserted rather than critically weighed
Conceptual distinctions (such as value versus truth in art) are not fully unpacked
The notion of fragility remains at times binary rather than positioned on a nuanced continuum
Broader implications for knowledge communities and methodological reflection are only briefly explored
Concluding synthesis lacks a clear weighing of factors across both areas of knowledge
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Given the examinerâs confirmation of a full-mark performance, the essay demonstrates an overall excellence in understanding and addressing the knowledge question. The student maintains a sustained, explicit focus on the prescribed title, develops clear and logically sequenced arguments, and employs accurate, well-explained examples from both areas of knowledge. There is evident awareness and some evaluation of different perspectives. While there are areas for further deepeningâespecially in nuanced evaluation and conceptual precisionâthe strengths of the essay justify allocation of the full 10 marks as per the examinerâs objective total.

annotations.comment: The Yamabe example is strong; however, you should analyse how typical partial proofs are in mathematics and their broader impact on community trust.
The conclusion summarises effectively but lacks a synthesis that evaluates the relative weight of factors across both areas of knowledge.

The definition of 'revered knowledge' conflates tradition and authority without acknowledging epistemic criteria; consider refining this to distinguish between social prestige and evidence-based justification.
The thesis statement lacks an explicit claim about the degree or continuum of fragility; the student should state their position clearly to guide the argument.
The concept of 'fragility' remains vague; suggest operationalizing fragility (e.g., frequency of rejection events or degree of epistemic disruption) to improve analytical precision.
Referencing 'appendix A' in the middle of your argument shifts focus to supplemental material; integrate critical evidence directly into the main text to strengthen your analysis.
Assertion that peer review strengthens knowledge oversimplifies the risk of collective bias; consider discussing instances of groupthink to balance the argument.
The claim that foundational knowledge remains revered despite disproof needs evidence; include examples of disproved foundational results to substantiate this.
Good discussion of partial proofsâ doubt-casting, but missing analysis of meta-epistemic safeguards (e.g., replication protocols) that mitigate this fragility.
This overview of factors is largely descriptive. The student should signpost their line of reasoning and indicate where critical evaluation of each factor will occur.
The claim about revering different versions treats fragility as binary; discuss how multiple versions may coexist on a spectrum of acceptance rather than absolute reverence.
The 'Last Supper' example is well chosen but remains descriptive; link restoration errors directly to potential shifts in collective acceptance to deepen the epistemic critique.
Here 'value' is equated with 'truth' in art without unpacking the distinction between aesthetic and epistemic validity; clarify these concepts to avoid conflation.
Consider probing the tension between reverence for foundations and the shock to the community when a core theorem is overturned to deepen critical insight.
The student overlooks the view that restoration debates can enhance scholarly discourse; include this counterpoint to provide a balanced evaluation of fragility.
The binary treatment of fragility in the arts overlooks gradations of audience reception; propose treating fragility as a continuum to add nuance to the discussion.
This evaluation of how reputation affects knowledge is insightful but lacks explicit connection to the core knowledge question; draw that link to enhance coherence.
The transition to mathematics is abrupt; a clear signpost would help the reader follow the shift between areas of knowledge more smoothly.
Describing the 'Enormous Theorem' proof length is useful, but you should explain how such complexity might conceal errors and thus increase fragility.
Noting that proof is tedious is valid, but the student should clarify how procedural complexity specifically contributes to knowledge fragility.
Fragility is still treated as a binary outcome; the student should discuss how degrees of fragility might vary with context and evidence.
The final recommendation is vague; propose concrete strategies for knowers to assess and respond to fragility in revered knowledge.
Include the counter-view that successive proof improvements can increase confidence over time to create a more balanced evaluation of mathematical fragility.
There is no reflection on implications for future knowledge production or how this awareness of fragility might change methodological approaches.
The conclusion does not explicitly revisit the core knowledge question; consider restating it to reinforce the essayâs overarching purpose.

reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a

the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
